name: Can new siding go over my old siding, or does everything need to be stripped off first?
headline: Can new siding go over my old siding, or does everything need to be stripped off first?

mainEntity.name: Can new siding go over my old siding, or does everything need to be stripped off first?
mainEntity.text: Can new siding go over my old siding, or does everything need to be stripped off first?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cladding over old siding can be done, but in this region it’s usually **safer to remove old siding** so you can inspect and repair what’s underneath. When you install new siding over old, you avoid demo mess and save some labor. But you also hide any existing problems—like rot, mold, or bad flashing—behind another layer. In coastal and foggy climates, those hidden issues often get worse and more expensive to fix later. Removing old siding lets you: - Check the sheathing and framing for water damage. - Add or repair housewrap and flashing around windows and doors. - Improve wall insulation or air sealing if the walls are open. There are also technical concerns. Extra layers can make walls too thick for window trim and flashing to work right. Fasteners may not penetrate deeply enough if you don’t adjust for the extra thickness. Local building codes sometimes limit how many layers you can have. If your existing siding is flat, sound, and free of moisture problems, and if the manufacturer allows installation over it, it can be an option on lower‑risk houses. But in older coastal homes, covering problems is a major pitfall. Always ask your installer for photos and documentation of any wall damage they find, and agree up front on how repairs will be priced.
